issue_title: Clean up Engine/Storage API
issue_body:
While removing Storage awareness from Scheduler, the API for ProductGraph walks and Engine root-product lookups got much more awkward.

While ProductGraph should definitely not be aware of Storage, user-facing methods of Engine/Scheduler should probably have facades that allow for (lazily?) executing Storage lookups during graph walks, so that users don't have to do it.

issue_title: Porting to Python 3
issue_body:
Hello, I'm an intern from Foursquare working with @mateor to migrate internally to Python 3, and one of our blockers is not being able to import Pants within Python3.

I'm hoping to be able to spearhead migrating Pants to Python 3 that is backwards compatible with 2.7.

## Scope
* Convert project source code to Python 3 .6
* Maintain library compatibility with Python 2.7

> "Attempting to write Python 3 code that’s compatible with Python 2 is much more rewarding than the opposite" ([Django authors](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/python3/#philosophy))

### Porting guides
[Official Python guide](https://docs.python.org/3/howto/pyporting.html)
[Python-future library](http://python-future.org/overview.html), tools and imports to maintain interopable codebase

## Tasks
- [ ] Run `futurize --stage1` for automatic and safe fixes; code still Python 2
- [ ] Port `setup` script & repo
- [ ] Enable Python3 virtualenv and interpreter under build-support
- [ ] Run `futurize --stage2` to switch over to Python 3; manually port what tool can't do
- [ ] Modify CI to ensure continuing Python 2.7 backwards compatibility
  - [ ] Investigate PyLint with `--py3k` arg
  - [ ] Investigate Tox to run CI in both 2.7 and 3.6

issue_title: Need secure handling of Android keystore credentials
issue_body:
This is an issue for the upcoming keystore/jarsigner commit:  https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/883/

The keystore target currently stores credentials in the BUILD files. It is paired with an entry in .gitignore to try and deter checking in credentials. But there are no checks in place to ensure that the credentials are put in the recommended BUILD.release file. In general, having passwords in a BUILD file is not ideal.

This is an issue to track the move to secure credential handling for Android targets. The idea is to move to a pluggable auth file, kept out of pants tree ideally. Machinery should be added to allow passing of credentials on CLI as well.

issue_title: [Feature Request] Partial Conformance with the SciPy Image I/O API
issue_body:
The long-heralded release of [SciPy 1.0.0](https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/release.1.0.0.html) has *finally* landed. Sixteen years was a hard wait, so this is a good thing.

## The Inevitable Bad News

Well, mostly good. SciPy 1.0.0 officially deprecates *all* image I/O functionality residing in the `scipy.ndimage` and `scipy.misc` packages. SciPy 1.2.0 is planned to **permanently remove** this functionality. Since [our downstream biophysics simulator](https://gitlab.com/betse/betse) heavily depends upon this functionality, this is sadly more of a bad than good thing for us.

[SciPy documentation](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.misc.imread.html) explicitly recommends `imageio` as a suitable replacement. Since the `imageio` API is *considerably* more bare bones than the equivalent SciPy APIs, however, `imageio` may prove to be a less-than-suitable replacement for some end users – notably, us.

## The Overly Optimistic Hope

Ideally, the `imageio` API could be marginally improved so as to provide at least *partial* support for several of the more commonly leveraged optional features of the SciPy image APIs – including:

* **Pillow-based mode conversion.** The [`scipy.ndimage.imread()`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.misc.imread.html) function, for example, provides an optional `mode` string parameter permitting external callers to specify a desired target mode (defaulting to `None`). If non-`None` and different from the image's current `mode`, this parameter is passed as is to the `PIL.Image.convert()` method.
* **Grayscale flattening.** Again, the [`scipy.ndimage.imread()`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.misc.imread.html) function provides an optional `flatten` boolean parameter permitting external callers to reduce non-grayscale images to grayscale (defaulting to `False`). If `True`, the `mode` parameter is coerced to `F`. Why not `L`? I have no idea and doubt that SciPy developers do either. I'm also not particularly fond of the name of this boolean; it *really* should have been something less ambiguous (e.g., `grayscale`, `as_gray`).

This isn't simply syntactic sugar.

This is critical. <sup>_Well, O.K. The `flatten` parameter is blatantly syntactic sugar._</sup>

External callers have *no* explicit access to the `PIL.Image` instance internally instantiated by this function. These optional parameters are the only means callers have of intervening in the conversion process.

I accept that `imageio` is not, will never be, and *should* never be an image processor. `imageio` is a pure image I/O framework – just as Code Jesus intended. Still, the thin line between image I/O and image processing is an easy Rubicon to cross – especially when Pillow already performs all of the heavy lifting on our behalf.

## Bold Details for a Bright Future

SciPy's image I/O is unconditionally implemented in terms of Pillow.

`imageio`, on the other hand, is conditionally implemented in terms of Pillow and whatever else it happens to scrounge from the local filesystem (e.g., FreeImage). Presumably, this conditionality prevents the optional `mode` and `flatten` (...err, `grayscale`?) parameters from being appended to the existing `imageio.imread()` API.

Would it be feasible, however, to permit these parameters to be:

* Added to the variadic `**kwargs` dictionary accepted by the `imageio.imread()` API.
* Passed to the `PIL.Image.convert()` method. In this case, a `True` value for the `grayscale` key should probably be munged into a `mode` value of either `L` or `F`.
* Processed in some fashion by the non-Pillow plugins. Hopefully, they won't simply be silently ignored – but I'll take what I can get, at the moment.

The `imageio.plugins.pil_get_frame()` function appears to offer similar functionality, albeit in a manner *not* exposed to external callers. Or is it? My modest (read: *awful*) `grep` skills failed me here.

Alternately, would exposing the `PIL.Image` instance internally instantiated by Pillow plugins via some public variable or getter method of the `imageio.core.format.Reader` class be feasible? I half-suspect this class *already* exposes `PIL.Image` instances, but (again) shamefully failed to `grep` the exact variable or method for doing so.

## The End of a Dream

That's about it, friendly `imageio` developers. Thanks for the long years of grueling work. May there be many more to come.

class LemmaTokenizer:
    def __init__(self):
        self.wnl = WordNetLemmatizer()
    def __call__(self, text):
        tokens = [self.wnl.lemmatize(t) for t in word_tokenize(text)]
        return tuple(tokens)

issue_title: add: remove_sonos_playlist
issue_body:
There are currently a couple of ways to create a sonos playlist but no way to delete them.  I am getting ready to do a PR to add this functionality.  I do have a couple questions and figured it best to get them sorted here before I create the PR.

1) parameter name - the underlying contentDirectory.DestroyObject call requires an 'object_id' to be set. I have the function signature looking like this: `remove_sonos_playlist(self, object_id)`  The content of object_id should be the be the item_id (i.e. SQ:10) of the playlist to delete.  object_id and item_id are used in the code and I am not sure if the signature should be object_id or item_id.  I guessed object_id since that is what the underlying call is using.

2) Proper position in the SoCo class doc string for the new function.  Methods and Properties are split out but within those subgroups it is not strictly alpha order.  It appears to be sub-grouped by functionality and then alpha within that.  I have it listed after add_item_to_sonos_playlist in the docstring and within the code.  Ok? OR Should it come after the two create_... methods?

3) Testing - against the mock soco object only or against the mock and an actual device (--ip)? I am unsure if the project is trying to go completely mock or if the mock usage is just to augment testing when you don't have a reachable device.

4) doc strings and docs - I see a variety of doc string/documenting methods in play.  Which is the preferred version?  The method I used for to_dict @KennethNielsen behaved rather badly when my parameter doc string needed to wrap and I had to butcher it to make it fit on one line within the length limit of pyflake/pep8. I am pretty sure it was my formatting or some such, but I was unsure of where to read up on the requirements to make the output work as intended. 

Best,
Jeff

issue_title: Unkown Attribute Error when listing queue from some music services
issue_body:
get_queue in core.py often fails with an Attribute error when a track from soundcloud is on the playlist.

Replacing
```python
track.creator,
track.title,
track.album,
```
with 

```python
getattr(track, "creator", ""),
getattr(track, "title", ""),
getattr(track, "album", ""),
```

fixes the problem, but this is a bit messy.

I'm happy to put in a PR for this, but would like suggestions as what people think is the best to fix it.

issue_title: Resolved value for ContentNodeResource.getCollection turns progress_fraction to Promise
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

Here's how I observed this

1. View a Lesson (no problem here)
1. View a Lesson Resources
1. Exit back to the Lesson Playlist
1. Observe this error:

```
vue.runtime.esm.js:587 [Vue warn]: Invalid prop: type check failed for prop "progress". Expected Number, got Promise.

found in

---> <ContentCard> at kolibri/plugins/learn/assets/src/views/content-card/index.vue
       <LessonPlaylistPage> at kolibri/plugins/learn/assets/src/views/classes/LessonPlaylistPage.vue
         <AppBody> at kolibri/core/assets/src/views/app-body.vue
           <CoreBase> at kolibri/core/assets/src/views/core-base.vue
             <Root>
```

At this line 

https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/develop/kolibri/plugins/learn/assets/src/state/actions/classesActions.js#L94,

the resolved value for contentNodes has one entry (the resource I actually viewed) where `contentNode.progress_fraction` is of type Promise instead of type Number.

In this situation, I can fix this by force fetching from `ContentNodeResource.getCollection()`.

### Expected behavior

`contentNode.progress_fraction` always is a Number.


### Context

Develop branch

issue_title: Replay system starts successfully but return error to web UI when being pass something other than a CDXJ
issue_body:
InterPlanetary Wayback 0.2017.07.04.0020

```
$ ipwb replay test3.warc.gz
Executing first-run procedure on provided sample data.
 * Running on http://127.0.0.1:5000/ (Press CTRL+C to quit)
```
Accessing http://127.0.0.1:5000/ in a browser produces the follow on the CLI for replay
```
Retrieving URI-Ms from test3.warc.gz
getting index file at /test3.warc.gz
string index out of range
127.0.0.1 - - [08/Jul/2017 17:11:57] "GET / HTTP/1.1" 500 -
127.0.0.1 - - [08/Jul/2017 17:11:58] "GET /serviceWorker.js HTTP/1.1" 200 -
```

and only a message that says, "Error" in the browser. Though the argument directed to an invalid source (a warc.gz.cdxj likely does not contain CDXJ data), there ought to be failsafes in-places to alert the user that the file passed to the replay system is not of the expected file type.

Further, the replay web UI ought to respond more gracefully than echo'ing the message "Error".

issue_title: Unicode hack destroys indentation
issue_body:
I was trying to use this tool on a `setup.py` file with valid syntax.
```python
# -*- coding: UTF-8 -*-
from distutils.core import setup

if foo:
    # just for a test with indentation
    bar()

setup(
    name=u'prospector-test-4-üéø',
    version='0.0.1',
    install_requires=[
        'Django==1.5.0',
        'django-gubbins==1.1.2'
    ]
)
```

Unfortunately [this hack](https://github.com/landscapeio/requirements-detector/blob/4ef161d4256a41abf51682973222a268aa7f44a2/requirements_detector/detect.py#L61) destroys the line indentation, and therefore produces invalid python code. This leads to an exception in AstroidBuilder (being catched [here](https://github.com/landscapeio/requirements-detector/blob/4ef161d4256a41abf51682973222a268aa7f44a2/requirements_detector/detect.py#L211)). 
Since parsing `setup.py` yielded an error, it falls back to parse `requirements.txt` - which in my case is empty.

**Proposed solution:**
Don't remove leading whitespace during `_load_file_contents`. I would have tried working on a fix myself, but I didn't quite understand the unicode hack involved here - I didn't want to break anything else...
Or is it enough to simply remove the `.strip()` from [here](https://github.com/landscapeio/requirements-detector/blob/4ef161d4256a41abf51682973222a268aa7f44a2/requirements_detector/detect.py#L61)?

issue_title: Change the condor job file writer so that BLAST XML never hits the disk
issue_body:
With #308 now solved, we can change `write-htcondor-job-spec.py` so that BLAST writes XML to stdout and that is directly consumed by `convert-blast-xml-to-json.py` which can now directly write bzip2 output.

Given the way condor works, I think this will need to be done via a new shell script that sets up the blast | convert pipeline and then touches an output file when it's all over. The output file gets created in this way (as we do currently with a .done file) because if we list the `.json.bz2` file as the output condor will think the job is done as soon as the file is created (i.e., before our pipeline has finished filling the file). We need a new script because the condor job file expects to run a single executable with a set of arguments, not a pipeline. We also have to pass the output filename to the new script. That's easily done, though the easiest ways aren't that pretty (e.g., add it to the command line and take it out before calling BLAST, or add it to the environment).

issue_title: get_from_cache() fails if redirection is required
issue_body:
**Description**
Trying PR #1364 (not merged yet) with ``pretrained_file`` equal to an official Glove URL, I got an exception from ``get_from_cache()``. The issue applies to master branch as well:
```
~/Repo/allennlp/allennlp/common/file_utils.py in get_from_cache(url, cache_dir)
    104     response = requests.head(url)
    105     if response.status_code != 200:
--> 106         raise IOError("HEAD request failed for url {}".format(url))
    107 
    108     # add ETag to filename if it exists

OSError: HEAD request failed for url http://nlp.stanford.edu/data/glove.6B.zip
```
The response status code is [302](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTP_302)  ("redirection found").

**To Reproduce**
```
from allennlp.common.file_utils import get_from_cache
get_from_cache('http://nlp.stanford.edu/data/glove.6B.zip')
```

**Possible solution**

Unless there's a particular reason to disallow redirections, the issue is easily fixed simply allowing them in line 104:
```
response = requests.head(url, allow_redirects=True)
```

issue_title: Tearing down a workspace can raise errors
issue_body:
If the server you're running removes files from inside the workspace dir when it's killed (like pid files, log files, sockets, etc), then the call to `shutil.rmtree` can raise exceptions. It appears that it's a race condition; rmtree lists all the files and then remove them, and in between the list and remove, the file can be removed by another process.

Passing `ignore_errors=True` should be enough to handle this. I've done this manually in my code by overriding `teardown` and it seems to work fine.

If you'd like that as a default I can put in a PR for it.

This is specifically problematic because it results in pytest reporting a failure, which fails our CI build.

issue_title: Delete Logical Drive doesn't take effect
When numbers become LONG search won't match records
issue_body:
If data being searched contains numeric values that are large enough to become LONG (depends on sys.maxint of the platform) then jmespath.search can't find them. To illustrate lets consider the following simple query that works:

```
>>> jmespath.search('[?b > `2`]', [{"a": 1, "b": 2}, {"a": 1, "b": 3}])
[{'a': 1, 'b': 3}]

```
Then simply convert the numeric data type of the field being searched to long and note the result is no longer found:

```
>>> jmespath.search('[?b > `2`]', [{"a": 1, "b": 2}, {"a": 1, "b": 3L}])
[]

```

issue_title: docs: Add documentation for recovering Ceph from quorum failure
issue_body:
The resolution to #2914 was a manual recovery of the cluster because it had lost enough monitors due to the addition of new hosts and could no longer achieve quorum. We successfully recovered the cluster with just one host that was part of the original, healthy cluster.

The line of responsibility for this documentation is unclear, as the relevant documentation exists in several places within the Ceph documentation, but we have no expectation that Deis users are familiar with Ceph (and arguably shouldn't be), so I think it'd be helpful to provide some general steps and examples for cluster recovery.

ping @mgartner - he wanted to see this, since I was silently performing these tasks without reporting in for several nerve-wracking minutes ;)

The general steps are:

1. Trying `ceph -s` on all hosts and noticing that none ever connect to the cluster - instead, they show timeouts and/or monitor faults
2. In a `store-monitor` container, using the [admin socket](http://ceph.com/docs/master/rados/troubleshooting/troubleshooting-mon/#using-the-monitor-s-admin-socket) to query [mon_status](http://ceph.com/docs/master/rados/troubleshooting/troubleshooting-mon/#understanding-mon-status), identifying that there are enough stale entries to prevent Ceph from gaining quorum 
3. Stopping the platform with `deisctl stop platform` so components stop trying to write data to store (note that leaving the routers up will allow application containers to remain up, unaffected)
4. Cleaning up stale entries in `/deis/store/hosts` so that dead monitors are not written out to clients
5. Updating `/deis/store/monSetupLock` to point to the healthy monitor -- note that this isn't strictly necessary, as this value is only used if wiping clean and starting a fresh cluster from scratch with no data, but it's good cleanup
6. Start the healthy monitor and use the admin socket to get the current state of the cluster. 
  * Given the cluster state as the monitor sees it, use [monmaptool](http://ceph.com/docs/master/man/8/monmaptool/) to manually remove stale monitor entries from the monmap (i.e. `monmaptool --rm mon.<hostname> --clobber /etc/ceph/monmap`)
7. On the same host as the healthy monitor, get a shell in a store-monitor container without a monitor running (see #2925), and inject the prepared monmap into the monitor with `ceph-mon -i <hostname> --inject-monmap /etc/ceph/monmap`)
8. Start the monitor and ensure it achieves quorum by itself (use `ceph -s` and/or query mon_status on the admin socket)
9. Start the other monitors and ensure they connect
10. Start the OSDs with `deisctl start store-daemon`.
11. Observe the OSD map with `ceph osd dump`
  * For each OSD that is no longer with us, follow [Removing an OSD](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/add_remove_host/#removing-an-osd) -- take care to ensure that the data is relocated before marking another OSD as `out`.
12. Once the OSD map reflects the now-healthy OSDs, start the remaining store services in order:
  * `deisctl start store-metadata`
  * `deisctl start store-gateway`
13. Confirm that the cluster is healthy with the metadata servers added, and then start `store-volume` with `deisctl start store-volume`.
14. Start the remaining services with `deisctl start platform`

issue_title: Feature: Allow System & Application monitoring based on Graphite
issue_body:
Warning: I have the feeling there should already be a bug report about this (maybe even commented on by me?) but I couldn't find it (I only looked at open bug reports though...)

As a Deis System Administrator I want to see

* Global (VM) Performance statistics over time (memory, CPU Utilization, Network Usage, (free) disk space). Normally this can be done by any networking system (zabbix), but "auto" configuration should be central (eg adding a node should automatically use this system).
Bonus points if I can add alerts that trigger on certain tresholds..

As a Deis user
* Per container metrics. This includes Memory Usage, CPU Usage, Network Usage etc per container & also averages per container type (web, worker, cmd, ...).
* Per web metrics. On top of that for the web type containers I also want metrics on requests (number of requests, response times, number of failed requests)...
This is similar to what Heroku provides. (https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/metrics)
* As bonus points I should be able to use the same toolkit for custom metrics (number of logins, ...)

I think graphite together with collectd / statsd and others will be a perfect method to implement this.

Obviously all of this would probably be implemented as optional components that can just run onto the coreos cluster...


Note: I Understand it is not in Deis' scope to write a new monitoring tool that implements storing metrics, triggers etc... I merely propose using existing tools, whereby I think Graphite based stack might be a good start (althoug something else can probably work as well).


If there is any intrest in this I might have a look at it mystelf to build out a basic platform...

issue_title: fix(controller): generate_app_name should re-roll on duplicate name
issue_body: Lurking through old comments, I found https://github.com/deis/deis/issues/475#issuecomment-32907618. If we find an existing app name when generating a random app name, we should re-roll `generate_app_name()` until we find a new name for the application, otherwise we will hit a 500 error because the keyspace in etcd already exists.

issue_title: Proposal: Enable opt-in HTTPS between router and app containers
issue_body:
See issue #4611 

Deis currently uses "mullet TLS--" security in the front, party in the back.  Having come from healthcare, I understand all too well how this rules out the possibility of using Deis to host any workloads that deal with PHI.  This is a gap that needs to be closed.

This issue isn't meant to duplicate #4611, but rather to serve as a forum for discussing proposed designs that address this.  I'll start by throwing my proposal into the ring.  Feedback is welcome, as are alternate proposals.

Approach
-------------

I think we can pretty easily go 80/20 on this and get ourselves most of the way to where we need to be pretty quickly and then improve incrementally from there going forward.  Initially, I'd propose that we support the the following, at minimum:

* 100% opt-in on an application basis so as not to impact existing, deployed applications upon platform upgrade.
* _Option_ for end-to-end HTTPS needs to be there.  If when selecting this option, it proves infeasible to _close_ the HTTP route into the same application in the first iteration, so be it.
* Self-signed certificates are good enough, initially.  It's only the router that needs to trust the certs-- not the end user's agent (since Deis can already terminate TLS at the router using a proper cert provided by the operator, signed by a known and trusted CA).

Possible implementation
---------------------------------

I believe operators should opt-in to this on an application basis by setting an environment variable via `deis config:set`.

The following is all WRT to buildpack-based applications:

One possible implementation is to generate a self-signed certificate at build-time.  I imagine the easiest way of doing this being modifications to the slugbuilder such that after the buildpack(s) have generated the application's deployable artifacts, the generated cert and key are added to those right before compressing those artifacts into a slug (tarball).  A corresponding change to the slugrunner can start Nginx as a proxy in front of the application to terminate TLS.

Open questions and concerns
----------------------------------------

I think experimentation is the best way to gain insight into the following:

* Will application containers _all_ include a self-signed cert and Nginx?  Or just those that have opted into it?  This depends on the availability of relevant information (environment variable) at build time.  Fortunately, the environment variables for each application are _also_ inserted into etcd, so we probably do have access to this information at build-time.

* If one opts into end-to-end HTTPS, can we expose the secure port in the application containers _instead of_ the insecure port?  Or, initially, do we expose it _in addition to_ the insecure port?  Proposing we shoot for the former; settle for the latter if necessary (for the first iteration).

* How are routing and publishing affected?  Publishing may or may not be affected depending on the previous bullet.  Routing rules will be slightly different for anyone any app that opts into this.

* How does this affect health checks and liveliness checks?

* How does it work with k8s as the scheduler?  With k8s as the scheduler, publisher isn't really relevant.  Router proxies service URLs.  Those service URLs likely need to change for any app that has opted into this.

* How does this work for developers pushing their own Dockerfile or for those using `deis pull`?  My personal take is that anyone who's already exercising that level of control over their images is responsible for exposing the secure endpoint themselves.  We would just need to clearly document how the router plugs into that in such scenarios.

issue_title: config:push with .env doesn't handle export or double quotes
issue_body:
If you have environment variables in your .env file that are surrounded by double quotes or start with an export they aren't handled correctly when pushed to deis. For example:

export REDIS_URL="redis://localhost:6379"

ends up as

export REDIS_URL: "redis://localhost:6379"

Where "export REDIS_URL" is the key and the value is still surrounded by double quotes which can then cause things to fail when the config is referenced.

issue_title: Transactions not supported by Hortonworks ODBC driver for Hive
issue_body:
I get the following error when I try to connect to Hive:

```
DatabaseError: ODBC error
state: HYC00
native error code: 11470
message: [Hortonworks][ODBC] (11470) Transactions are not supported.
```

I can successfully connect with this same driver and DSN in `isql`.

My understanding is that this happens because turbodbc enforces explicit transactions with `Connection.commit`. Are there plans to add an autocommit option for `turbodbc.connect`? Perhaps that would alleviate errors like this.

Thanks!

issue_title: Whisper: test_shh_filter does not work to send messages to another node.
issue_body:
* Version: 4.4.1
* Python: 3.5
* OS: osx/linux

### What was wrong?

I have followed following ssh-mode code (https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/master/tests/core/shh-module/test_shh_filter.py), which is able to send message to itself but other nodes are not able to catch the sent messages. 

On this approach each node can only sends message to itself and other nodes don't filter/see those messages. For example, when `node-1` sends a message it won't show up on the `node-2` such that node-2's `web3.shh.info{memory}` remains 0, which I assume that it never catches the message.


### How can it be fixed?

Node_1 runs its filter_watch.

```
sender = web3.shh.newKeyPair()
sender_pub = web3.shh.getPublicKey(sender)

receiver = web3.shh.newKeyPair()
receiver_pub = web3.shh.getPublicKey(receiver)

topic = '0x13370000'
payloads = [web3.toHex(text="test message :)"), web3.toHex(text="2nd test message")]

shh_filter = web3.shh.newMessageFilter({
    'privateKeyID': receiver,
    'sig': sender_pub,
    'topics': [topic]
}, poll_interval=0.5)
watcher = shh_filter.watch(received_messages.extend)

print('sleeping')
time.sleep(15);
```

issue_title: Error in function Package.__lt__()
issue_body:
There are two problems in function `Package.__lt__()`

A. The code in [resolve.py:203](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/resolve.py#L203)
```python
            return ((self.norm_version, self.build_number, other.build) <
                    (other.norm_version, other.build_number, self.build))
```
should read
```python
            return ((self.norm_version, self.build_number, self.build) <
                    (other.norm_version, other.build_number, other.build))
```

B. When version numbers do not conform to [PEP 386](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0386/) (i.e. are not of the form `N.N[.N]+[{a|b|c|rc}N[.N]+][.postN][.devN]` with `N` representing natural numbers), the above comparison fails, and conda falls back to a lexicographic comparison between version strings ([resolve.py:206](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/resolve.py#L206)):
```python
            return ((self.version, self.build_number) <
                    (other.version, other.build_number))
```
This fall-back is a bad choice because it leads to unpleasant surprises when people are not aware of the concept of conforming version numbers. For example
```
1.0.9b < 1.0.10c => True  (both are conforming)
1.0.9b < 1.0.10d => False (1.0.10d is non-conforming)
```
I see two solutions:
* reject non-conforming versions numbers during `conda build`
* implement the fall-back comparison in terms of the rule 'numbers are compared numerically, strings of letters are compared lexicographically'

I highly favor the latter solution.

issue_title: Version pinning misses capability for Regular Expressions
issue_body:
I would like to restrict a package's version to release versions through pinning, excluding pre-release builds.
I think the only way to achieve this is using a regex for the version specification in the `pinned` file. But since there is no way to explicitly force a line in the pinned file to be marked a regex, [we can only get a regex-matching `VersionSpec` by satisfying this line in it's constructor](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/231e9db898b3d7720b49e9e2050a88be6978fd38/conda/version.py#L319):
```
    elif '*' in spec.rstrip('*'):
```
So we would have to put multiple stars in the version spec but make sure that the resulting regex is still what we want to match.. plus, there's all kind of implicit replacementsdone to our regex, so this is not feasible I think.

Consider this example:
```
((maintenance_1.0.x)) wintermute:~$ conda search basemap
Fetching package metadata ...........
basemap                      1.0.6                np17py27_0  defaults        
                          .  1.0.7               np110py27_0  defaults        
                             1.0.8.dev           np110py27_0  conda-forge/linux-64 
                          *  1.0.8.dev0          np110py27_1  conda-forge/linux-64 
```
Let's assume I want to pin to any release version build of `1.0` series, excluding the "*.dev*" pre-release packages. I don't see a way to do that..

How about something like..
```
basemap ^1\.0\.[0-9]+$
```
and an if/else clause in the (admittedly already pretty cluttered) constructor of `VersionSpec`:
```python
elif spec.startswith("^") and spec.ensdwith("$"):
    self.spec = spec
    self.regex = re.compile(spec)
    self.match = self.regex_match_
```
That would enable people to use Regex in `pinned` file (detected by expicit start/end of string markers)..

Hi,

Is there an easy/simple way to recover from an interrupted 'conda update conda'. Then if any of those packages fail to update, the whole process exits and my conda installation is broken. Is there a way to recover from this? 

P

issue_title: VersionSpec wildcard problem
issue_body:
I don't think `numpy 1.1*` should match `numpy 1.10`, but that's exactly what happens:
```
from conda.version import VersionSpec
vspec = VersionSpec('1.1*')
vspec.match('1.10')
```
This returns `True` but it should be `False`, in my view. 

I think it has been this way as long as the `VersionSpec` object has been around (I bisected to 3.14).

The reason for this is that `VersionSpec` converts `1.1*` to the regex `(1\.1.*)$`. (As far as I can tell the grouping is superfluous.) I think it should do something like this instead: `1\.1(?:\..*)?$`

Thoughts? @ukoethe

issue_title: Any plan of supporting conda for the non-scientific community?
issue_body:
We came across Anaconda yesterday, while trying to migratate our production s/w from py2 to py3. We like the ease of using it. #1 We installed miniconda3 and find that there are many packages installed that we don't want. For eg: ipython, ipykernel, jupyter, matplotlib, etc. We actually don't use these and would like to have an installation where these packages are not installed by default, more of a bare and clean environment to start with. Is that possible? #2 We find that tornado=5.1 is installed. For our production version, we use an older one, tornado=3.1. We installed it, however, there seems to be some path issues, and tornado 5.1 gets imported. >>> import tornado >>> tornado.version '5.1' How to install and use an older version of an already installed package? issue_title: Respecting topological sorting for uninstall
issue_body: Not sure if this is implemented in newer versions of `conda`, but it would be nice if package removal performed a topological sort and uninstalled packages based on this sorting. Namely only removing packages that are not required for anything else first. For the most part this doesn't matter, but when `pre-unlink` scripts are involved it does.

issue_title: Consider removing tab completion to improve startup time
issue_body:
Several users have recently noticed slow conda startup times.  It seems the major reason we load so much code at startup is to support argparse's tab completion.

See https://groups.google.com/a/continuum.io/forum/?utm_medium=email&utm_source=footer#!msg/conda/UU8MHIoGMqA/FqSy4SpSAQAJ for one recent discussion.

If the tradeoff is tab completion or startup time, my inclination is that most users would go for startup time.

Thoughts?

CC @msarahan @ilanschnell @mingwandroid

issue_title: Make pcapy Reader object selectable
pysam.libcbgzf.BGZFile object is not iterable
issue_body:
Im trying to read and write bgzfiles using pysam.libcbgzf.BGZFile but I get 

```TypeError: 'pysam.libcbgzf.BGZFile' object is not iterable```

Here is test code that can be run using pytest. Did I use it correctly or is this a bug?

using `pysam==0.10.0`

```
import pytest
import pysam
import gzip
import tempfile


@pytest.fixture(params=[True, False])
def input_as_row(request):
    return request.param


@pytest.fixture(params=["", "b"])
def as_binary(request):
    return request.param


@pytest.fixture(params=[
    "A\nB\nC\n",
    "\nAAA\t",
    "AAA",
    ""])
def input_data(request):
    return request.param


@pytest.fixture(params=[
    pysam.BGZFile,
    gzip.GzipFile,
    open])
def file_class(request):
    return request.param


def test_file(
        file_class,  # Different types of files
        input_data,  # Few different input strings
        input_as_row,  # Write rows or as one string
        as_binary):  # Open in binary mode
    output_data = ""
    with tempfile.TemporaryFile() as tmp:
        with file_class(tmp.name, "w{}".format(as_binary)) as f:
            if input_as_row:
                for row in input_data.splitlines(True):
                    f.write(row)
            else:
                for row in input_data:
                    f.write(row)

        with file_class(tmp.name, "r{}".format(as_binary)) as f:
            for row in f:
                output_data += row
    assert input_data == output_data
```

issue_title: feature request for changes to compiler check
issue_body:
The main reason for this feature request that I don't get the compiler to work when called via batch (the error level return seems borked). All these are rare issues.

The idea is the following:

* before test compilation: check if test destination exists, remove it (error if not possible) [this would lead to problems with the current implementation]
* use multiple status values for succes (the executable success and status is currently overwritten by the module check)
* after test compilation: check if destination exists: if no --> failed, if yes and return-code != 0 --> warning, if yes and return-code == 0 --> ok

The change would be in open_cobol_ide/compilers.py, (check_compiler) around line 251.

~~~~python

    def check_compiler(cls, compiler):
        def get_output_path(input_path):
            dirname, filename = os.path.split(input_path)
            basename = os.path.splitext(filename)[0]
            possible_extensions = ['.exe', '.bat', '']     # Simon: changed (not necessary for non-win at all)
            for ext in possible_extensions:
                candidate = os.path.join(dirname, basename + ext)
                if os.path.exists(candidate):
                    return candidate
            return 'none'

        from open_cobol_ide.view.dialogs.preferences import DEFAULT_TEMPLATE
        working_dir = tempfile.gettempdir()
        cbl_path = os.path.join(working_dir, 'test.cbl')
        with open(cbl_path, 'w') as f:
            f.write(DEFAULT_TEMPLATE)                      # Simon: What would happen here if the file can not be written?
        dest = os.path.join(tempfile.gettempdir(),
                            'test' + ('.exe' if system.windows else ''))

        _logger().debug('check compiler')

         # Simon: add something like this (via sub-function to remove duplicated code)
        if os.path.exists(dest):
            try:
                os.remove(dest)
            except OSError:
               # log something
           
        success1 = False
        status, output = run_command(compiler, ['-x', cbl_path],
                                     working_dir=working_dir)
        dest = get_output_path(cbl_path)
        if dest:
            if os.path.exists(dest)
                success1 = True
                if status != 0
                   # log/display warning
         
               # detect default executable extension
               GnuCobolCompiler.extensions[0] = os.path.splitext(dest)[1]
               try:
                   os.remove(dest)
               except OSError:
                   pass


         # Simon: add something like this (via sub-function to remove duplicated code)
        if os.path.exists(dest):
            try:
                os.remove(dest)
            except OSError:
               # log something
           
        success2 = False
        status, output = run_command(compiler, [cbl_path],
                                     working_dir=working_dir)
        dest = get_output_path(cbl_path)
        if dest:
            if os.path.exists(dest)
                success2 = True
                if status != 0
                   # log/display warning
         
               # detect default executable extension
               GnuCobolCompiler.extensions[1] = os.path.splitext(dest)[1]
               try:
                   os.remove(dest)
               except OSError:
                   pass

            # Simon: check both success values and only return the extension if the check worked
        _logger().info('GnuCOBOL compiler check: %s (%d/%d)',
                       'success' if success1 and success2 else 'fail',
                       success1, success2)
        if success1:
           _logger().info('Executable extension: %s' %
                          GnuCobolCompiler.extensions[0])

       if success2:
        _logger().info('Module extension: %s' %
                       GnuCobolCompiler.extensions[1])

        try:
            os.remove(cbl_path)
        except OSError:
            pass

        if not success:
            status = -1

        return output, status
~~~~

issue_body:

```
       LINKAGE SECTION.
```

issue_title: Make eapi.conf file format consistent with rbeapi
Testers Needed for Cadence Incisive and Xcelium
issue_body:
The core development team for VUnit does not have easy access to Cadence Incisive and Xcelium licenses which prevents us from running our acceptance tests on those simulators. To get a higher confidence in the updates we release we need feedback from the VUnit users using those simulators. What we're looking for is feedback in general and people that can run our acceptance tests on new release candidates. Our acceptance tests are part of the VUnit installation and running them is just a matter of starting a test suite from the command line to get a test report.

If you interested in the continuous support of these simulators you can comment on this issue or contact [me](https://github.com/LarsAsplund) directly.

issue_title: Deleting indices by priorities and space
issue_body:
Hi!
I have the following scenario: 
I want to delete indices when my cluster storage reaches a fixed water mark (for example - 1TB).
But, I want to delete indices by a certain order. I have 3 groups of indices with different priorities for deletion. 

First, I want to delete indices from the first group, from the oldest to the newest (but not newer than 1 month) until the total space of the cluster reaches the desired water mark. 
If the water mark is not reached, I want to continue and delete indices from the second group, from the oldest to the newest (but not newer than 3 months) until the total space of the cluster reaches the desired water mark. 
From the third group I never delete indices. 

I was not able to figure out how to implement this with curator python api. 

I tried to filter out all indices but the ones from the first group which are newer than 1 month, which I want to delete first. Then I would have liked to call `filter_by_space`. But to do that, I had to provide `disk_space` parameter, which is the desired space that this specific group of indices should take. This was inconvenient for me because I only know how much space should the total cluster take, not this specific group of indices.
So my solution was:
1. Calculate the amount of storage I have to delete.
2. Calculate the size of the group of loud indices
3. Reduce the size to delete from the size of the group (check that it is not below zero) and pass the result to `filter_by_space`.

This solution is not clean and requires many calculations. 

I would have liked to suggest adding `filter_by_space` would check how much space does the whole cluster take (and not the specific group of indices) OR add the possibility to receive `space_to_clean`, meaning - how much storage to delete, but maybe I am missing something. 

Do you have any suggestions how to handle this scenario better?

Thanks.

issue_title: user:password in hosts are not url decoded
issue_body:
Hello,

If I have a character like "]" in my username or password I must urlencode it or `urlparse` will interpret the host as IPv6. Unfortunately, `urlparse` does not urldecode fragments : 
```
The components are not broken up in smaller parts (for example, the network location is a single string), and % escapes are not expanded.
```
(https://docs.python.org/3/library/urllib.parse.html)

`_normalize_hosts` should urldecode explicitely, which I will propose in a PR.

issue_title: Validators need way to examine original command line in addition to namespace
issue_body:
Scenario:  VMSS create can create either a load balancer, application gateway, or neither.  Each (LB/AG) are mutually exclusive parameter sets--you can't create both.  Either one could have parameters in that parameter set that have default values.

The problem then is this.  If I have a default value, it *will always* appear in the validator's namespace. If I then write code which validates the mutual exclusivity of the set, it will always fail because of these default values.

My options given the current infrastructure:
1. Leave those parameters out of the validation. This goes against the principle that we should not simply ignore arguments but throw an error.
2. Validate these parameters, but allow them to succeed if the value is the same as the default.  This results in higher maintenance cost, but also results in silently ignoring the value if the user manually supplies a value that is the default.
3. Do some kind of horrible mangling with the argparse action kwarg that somehow mangles the value to indicate that it was actually user specified.  I tried this once and it was terrible and error prone. You have to remember to remove the mangling later on and it was just a nightmare.
4. Make the default values for these None and then have to manually specify it in the help text and manually set it in the code. This is not maintainable and confusing.

What I actually want is the ability to see the namespace as well as a namespace-like structure of the options the user *actually* specified on the command line. In this way, I can apply the validation only to the parameters the user actually specified (for evaluating usage errors) while retaining all the same benefits of specifying defaults the simple way.

This only affects our more complicated commands (creates/updates), but given that the most complicated commands tend to be the most important, this matters.

cc/ @johanste

issue_title: File state incorrectly marked as "errored" if contains chunks is "pending" state
issue_body:
### Description


in `_update` 

```
if cstates.contains_all('finished'):
...
elif cstates.contains_none('running'):              
    self._fstates[parent] = 'errored'
```
If chunks are in `pending` state instead, this will mark the parent state as "errored" which will subsequently cause `active` ( `return not self._fstates.contains_none('pending', 'transferring', 'merging')`) to return `False` and `monitor` to continue with chunks in `pending` / `running` states.

If by the time `run` checks the chunks of a file the chunk has finished, this will continue, if chunks are in `running` this will error.

---

### Reproduction Steps
Not a deterministic way, as it depends on parallel task execution, but either having many chunks or repeatedly will cause this (i.e. we need to 1) have chunks in pending state **and** 2) have some in running state when we check them)

```
def test_dummy(azure):
    def transfer(adlfs, src, dst, offset, size, blocksize, buffersize, shutdown_event=None):
        return size, None

    client = ADLTransferClient(azure, transfer=transfer, chunksize=8,
                               chunked=True)

    client.submit('foo', AzureDLPath('bar'), 32*32)
    client.run()
```

issue_title: Implement `default_language_version` mapping
issue_body:
This would allow you to blanket choose an interpreter version across all hooks implemented in that language.

This is described in some detail [here](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/644#issuecomment-339770094) though the rough idea would look something like:

```yaml
default_language_version:
    python: python2.7
    ruby: 2.1.5
```

This would give precedence to manually configured (either through `.pre-commit-hooks.yaml` or `.pre-commit-config.yaml`) versions and only overwrite in the case where `default` is not specified.  (precedence: manually specified > default_language_version > `language.get_default_version()`)

issue_title: pre-commit hook 'suites' or 'collections'
issue_body:
Disclaimer: An idea for a QoL thing I'm not fully convinced about the usefulness of myself.

**Intro**: We have several dozen repos that all use pre-commit. It can be a pain to manage the .pre-commit-config.yaml files in each of those repositories separately i.e. we may start or stop using some hooks across all repos and then it takes some effort to update all their .pre-commit-config.yaml files. I and others have various scripts and tools built to do mass updates across repositories, but I was thinking about whether there was a simpler way to maintain and think about this.

**Idea**: Versioned 'suites' or 'collections' of hooks.
Imagine a repo `pre_commit_hook_collections` containing some directory `collections/`:
```
collections/
  python_package_hooks.yaml
  service_hooks.yaml
  frontend_service_hooks.yaml
  ...
```
Each of those is a pre-commit config file with the same format as .pre-commit-config.yaml. python_package_hooks.yaml for example might have all the basic hooks we want in every single python package (line ending fixer, pep8, no large files, etc). When we create a new python package we would have it's .pre-commit-config.yaml file look like:
```
-   repo: git@git.example.com:pre-commit/pre-commit-hook-collections
    sha: e9446ff0cc8ffa65309e0a37c247518127a3bf18
    collections:
    -   id: python-package-hooks
        language_version: python3.6
```

`pre-commit autoupdate` would bump the version of hook collections same as it bumps version of hooks now.

Having a repo's .pre-commit-config.yaml include collection(s) does not mean it can not also specify additional regular precommit hooks. For example, if we add some yaml file to our python package we might specifically add a yaml validator or sorter hook to it. 

When we want to add/remove hooks across many repositories it then becomes a job of updating the collections repository with those changes. Then as package authors across the team or multiple teams update their hooks they'll get those changes. 

Looking for thoughts on the usefulness of this idea, and feasibility.

issue_title: Support using existing Docker images as hooks
issue_body:
Currently a `language: docker` hook only supports running a Docker image which it built itself (via a `Dockerfile`).

However this seems a little unnecessary and expensive since we can and should be building and publishing our Docker images to a registry.

Would there be interest in supporting using existing images as hooks? It should be simple and I'd be happy to make the PR.

## Proposed `.pre-commit-hooks.yaml` change

```yaml
-   id: flake8-docker
    name: flake8 (via docker)
    description: flake8, but provided by docker
    files: \.py$

    language: docker
    # Image to `docker pull`
    docker_image: my.registry.example.com/pre-commit-hooks/flake8:latest
    # Optional: use `ENTRYPOINT` of the image if not specified
    entry: flake8
```

issue_title: Enabling SSE potentially slows down performance(?)
issue_body:
I'm experimenting with the Bullet physics engine version 2.82, and comparing scalar code implementation to SSE implementation (`BT_USE_SSE` and `BT_USE_SSE_IN_API` defined). My tests are being run on a Windows Haswell system with Visual Studio 2015 compiler. The scene consists of 1500 boxes falling from the sky in six tall columns. You can find an interactive HTML5 web build of the scene here: https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/40949268/emcc/2015-10-05-emunittest_0.4-PhysicsStressTest-Urho3D_1.4_e1.34.11-release-prof/12_PhysicsStressTest.html (please run in Firefox, preferably in 64-bit and in Nightly for best performance). Note that the web build is just for illustration purposes of what my scene looks like. I am not benchmarking the web build, but a native Windows 64-bit build.

The results I am getting are quite surprising: when I compile a 32-bit Windows executable with `BT_USE_SSE` and `BT_USE_SSE_IN_API` defined, I am getting roughly 10% *worse* performance compared to the scalar build. When comparing 64-bit builds, SSE is a bit faster than scalar, but only about 3% (tested one run, not much for statistical significance). I am profiling the hotspots with AMD CodeXL, and in each case, Bullet functions are consuming more than 90% of the total frametime, so the 3D engine driving the application should not be affecting the results. Looking at AMD CodeXL profiles confirms that the workload is saturated with Bullet simulation:

   - scalar: https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/40949268/dump/Urho3D_scalar.png
   - sse: https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/40949268/dump/Urho3D_sse.png

In the web build, that can be confirmed by hitting F2, and observing the times given by the internal profiler, "StepSimulation" is where Bullet physics is being driven.

I have fixed the timesteps I pass to `stepSimulation`, calling it at a constant rate of timeStep = 0.016667, maxSubSteps=0, internalTimeStep = 0.016667.

My question is whether there exists benchmarks of Bullet's sse performance posted somewhere? Are my results to be expected?

issue_title: Documentation reorganizing
issue_body:
aiohttp doc was started from very few pages with relative small content on each.
Now it grows.
Take a look on *Client Usage* for example: http://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/client.html 
Endless list of topics in navigation bar and infinite page to scroll on the right.

The same is true for web server docs, the situation is even worse.

The proposal is:
1. Shrink navbar to top livel topics only: client, server, misc (the last is a subject for discussion).
2. Client should contain `Client Usage` and `Client Reference` but we need split the Usage into several pages like "Basics", "Cookie Support", "File Sending" etc. I don't know the splitting criteria, it is a hard choice -- but we need it.
3. The same for `Client Reference` but the splitting is more obvious: ClientSession, request and response, connectors and DNS resolvers.
4. Server part: Tutorial, Usage and Reference, Logging, Low-Level Server and Deployment
5. Others

@aio-libs/aiohttp-committers I need your suggestions and help

issue_title: Notebook can only be used on root URL directory /
issue_body:
All of the links in notebook are hardcoded to the root / of the URL.

So basically, it expects that the notebook is being run at: localhost:8888/

I have been trying to tunnel notebook through a nginx reverse proxy, so that I can access the notebooks at: localhost/p/

However, this does not work, as notebook does not acknowledge that it is being loaded at localhost/p/ as the base URL.

For example, when I try to load localhost/p/, notebook automatically redirects me to localhost/tree (instead of localhost/p/tree). Where localhost/p/api should work, notebook will only call localhost/api.

This means that notebook is impossible to incorporate into a larger system, as it demands a number of very important directories! It demands /static, /api, /custom, among others.

If notebook removed the leading "/" from its calls, making it location agnostic, then it would be easier to incorporate it into a larger system. e.g. localhost/p/ for notebook, localhost/r/ for rstudio, localhost/prod/ for production shiny apps, etc etc.

issue_title: Local core should speak HTTPS to node.blockstack.org
issue_body:
Interactions between local core node and node.blockstack.org should be over HTTPS. While secrets are not communicated over this link, interactions like name lookups and price lookups are, which leaks potentially information about how a given user is interacting with the network as well as information about names the user may be considering registering.

This can be fixed directly by getting our proxy service for node.blockstack.org a certificate and updating the client to allow URLs for connections to the blockstack indexers (this will allow us to specify "https").

issue_title: blockstackd: move subdomain DB to blockstackd
issue_body:
Right now, `blockstack_client` lazily builds up the `subdomains.db` file when it processes a subdomain query.  Instead, `blockstackd` should eagerly build up this database as it receives zone files.

In particular, we should treat subdomains like normal names whenever possible.

issue_title: Does this library provide Last.fm authentication callback support?
issue_body:
I am using Last.fm to authenticate users in a Django application and expect Last.fm to send my application a [callback with a temporary authentication token](http://www.last.fm/api/webauth). I am unclear how to do this with `pylast`. The closest API appears to be [SessionKeyGenerator. get_web_auth_session_key(url)](https://github.com/pylast/pylast/blob/7b3ab250df752caeba712b48be1e760bfdbc9983/pylast/__init__.py#L1307), but that doesn't actually extract the token from the callback URL.

I ended up doing it manually using `_Request`:

```python
token = request.GET.get('token')  # Django request  object

network = pylast.LastFMNetwork(
    api_key=LASTFM_API_KEY,
    api_secret=LASTFM_API_SECRET,
)

call = pylast._Request(network, 'auth.getSession', {'token': token})
call.sign_it()

try:
    xml = call.execute()
except pylast.WSError as exc:
    log.error(exc)
    return None
else:
    return xml
```

Did I miss an API that provides similar functionality? Would you consider a PR that provides this functionality?

issue_title: Add a way to check that mapping score goes up per read
issue_body:
I'm still trying to work out how to attach a per-read mapping score test to the Jenkins CI setup. It looks like the right place for the score extraction and comparison logic might be inside of toil-vg's `mapeval` mode. We're already using it to run alignments of the same reads against all the different kinds of graphs in the Jenkins tests, to make sure that the mapping positions are accurate. It wouldn't be too much harder to also feed `mapeval` the information that the mapping scores against one graph should always exceed the mapping scores against another graph. Alternately, we could tell it which graph to compare against, and it could spit out per-read score difference data for each other graph, and then the Jenkins test could scan through that and complain if any of the differences are negative.

@cmarkello, does this design make sense given what toil-vg's `mapeval` mode is supposed to do? Are you OK with me trying to turn it into a more general mapping evaluator, rather than just a tool to look at mapping positions?

issue_title: In some situations TCP traffic gets dropped due to the check for TTL 42
issue_body:
I ran into a problem where a specific HTTPS server didn't respond most of the time, only in very specific environments.  I traced down the problem to the TTL 42 hack that allows running the sshuttle client and server on the same machine.  Removing that hack allowed the HTTPS server to respond back (as packets are no longer dropped).  

I would suggest to add an option: --ttl-hack  (disabled by default) that could be used to run sshuttle client and server on the same machine.  This way, by default sshuttle would work as expected.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Adjust the implprefix DeprecationWarning's stack level
issue_body:
Hi!

In #144 a deprecation warning was added for usage of the `implprefix` argument.

However the `warnings.warn()` call doesn't set a `stacklevel`, which means when the warning is output, the blamed line is in pluggy, rather than the plugin that's using `implprefix`. For example:
https://travis-ci.org/mozilla/treeherder/jobs/409417404#L895

Passing `stacklevel=2` would make it clearer as to where the issue lies, and also mean the correct module path can be referenced in eg pytest's `filterwarnings` (for downstream projects who enable warnings/deprecation warnings as errors).

Many thanks :-)

CC @tgoodlet

issue_title: More flexibility on Remote driver host address
issue_body:
Would you be open to a PR that would allow for a port value like -1 that would remove the colon and port number `:port` from the constructed url?   I desire to host a grid at an endpoint like `http://mygrid.mycompany.com/wd/hub`
https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-selenium/blob/2c40b5c395162f598ed02e7302d3ed4d71a0873b/pytest_selenium/drivers/remote.py#L12

issue_title: Remote Firefox driver passes incorrect capabilities
issue_body:
Using the latest 3.7.0 docker selenium firefox image and pytest-selenium 1.11.1 I get an error that states ```unable to find matching set of capabilities```. 

I removed pytest selenium and followed the remote driver instructions found here: http://selenium-python.readthedocs.io/getting-started.html#using-selenium-with-remote-webdriver and I was able to get the tests to pass again.

I am thinking the remote capabilities that pytest-selenium copies has been outdated, or maybe the selenium has changed the data type.

issue_title: goog:chromeOptions from capabilities json are ignored when remote driver is used
issue_body:
I want to run my tests using a remote driver in headless chrome browser. I'm using pytest-variables to pass capabilities.json as capabilities for the driver but the browser is not being run in headless mode (chrome window is opened every time). 

I think the json is in a correct format as it is working when I use json with selenium driver directly. That's why I started suspecting that  the issue is in pytest-selenium. 

While I was going through the code I found that in "capabilities" fixture inside pytest_selenium/pytest_selenium.py before the block handling remote driver class(from line 59 to line 72) the goog:chromeOptions are in the capabilities variable but after the block they are removed  from capabilities. 

Im trying to understand the "if" block on pytest_selenium/pytest_selenium.py:70: 

https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-selenium/blob/171321caed510f6b35108d743eea4b733207f177/pytest_selenium/pytest_selenium.py#L70-L71

for example when CHROME is the browser both key and options are set resulting in all([key, options]) returning "True" and thus executing the capabilities.setdefault... What I don't understand is what is it supposed to do? Right now it rewrites the capabilities that are loaded from the json with defaults. (as we see in the "capabilities" after the block). Shouldn't it work the other way around and combine the default values with the capabilities provided in the json? Am I missing something or is there something wrong in my setup? 


**Run command:**
`polka$ pytest ./tests/test_login.py --driver=Remote --variables=./capabilities_chrome.json`

**Platform and pytest plugins:**
- platform darwin -- Python 3.6.1, pytest-3.4.1, py-1.5.2, pluggy-0.6.0
- plugins: xdist-1.22.2, variables-1.7.1, selenium-1.11.4, metadata-1.6.0, html-1.16.1, forked-0.2, base-url-1.4.1

**Contents of capabilities_chrome.json:**
`{
  "capabilities": {
    "browserName": "chrome",
    "platform": "Mac",
    "goog:chromeOptions": {
      "args": [
        "headless"
      ]
    }
  }
}`


**Before the block, capabilities are:**
`"{'browserName': 'chrome', 'platform': 'Mac', 'goog:chromeOptions': {'args': ['headless']}}"`

**After the block, capabilities are:** 
`"{'browserName': 'chrome', 'platform': 'Mac', 'goog:chromeOptions': {'args': [], 'extensions': []}}"`

issue_title: Specify Firefox binary location
issue_body:
New Firefox versions require `geckodriver` for Selenium tests. However this Marionette based driver is not feature complete and not everything that use to work is functional anymore.

The solution is to downgrade Firefox. However if you run normal Firefox on the same computer there is a confusion about which Firefox binary to use. 

I'd like to provide a patch that allows you to specify the location of Firefox binary. Would you have any guidance for this what parts to touch?

issue_title: loading existing cluster with devices fails
issue_body:
When creating a cluster, and then instantiating a new ClusterManager object, the code fails with UnexpectedDeviceGroupDevices.

This is because the partition is not being taken in to consideration when creating the given_device_names list

issue_title: Default price faceted search does not work with 1.6
issue_body:
### Issue Summary

The price based faceted search (http://django-oscar.readthedocs.io/en/releases-1.1/ref/settings.html#oscar-search-facets) does not work with the current sandbox site 

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Follow the "Running Sandbox locally" guide at http://django-oscar.readthedocs.io/en/releases-1.1/internals/sandbox.html#run-the-sandbox-locally
2. Update the settings.py file with a solr or elasticsearch 2.4 haystack setting
3. Start the runserver, browse to a category and try to use the filters.

I understand that there is work afoot to switch to our own ES backed search engine but at the moment these filters just do not work as advertised

### Technical details

* Python version: 3.6.5
* Django version: 2.0.6.
* Oscar version: 2.0.dev0

issue_title: Alpine end to end support checklist
issue_body:
This issue keeps track of progress on adding end to end Alpine support. As the comment https://github.com/dotnet/coreclr/issues/917#issuecomment-344163031 shows, the transparency of what was done and what needs to be done yet was low, so this issue tries to fix that.
- [x] Enable build of coreclr native components on Alpine
- [x] Enable build of corefx native components on Alpine
- [x] Enable build of core-setup native components on Alpine
- [x] Create Alpine bootstrap CLI and upload it to Azure so that the coreclr, corefx, core-setup repos can use it for building managed components
- [x] Enable build of coreclr managed components on Alpine
- [x] Enable build of corefx managed components on Alpine
- [x] Enable build of core-setup managed components on Alpine
- [x] Enable official build of coreclr nuget packages for Alpine
- [x] Enable official build of corefx nuget packages for Alpine
- [x] Enable official build of core-setup nuget packages for Alpine

At this point, Alpine Linux can be targeted by CLI on non-Alpine platforms, but not much testing was done yet.
- [x] Enable CI tests for Alpine in coreclr
- [x] Enable CI tests for Alpine in corefx
- [x] Enable CI tests for Alpine in core-setup
- [x] Enable test lab runs of coreclr unit tests for Alpine
- [x] Enable test lab runs of corefx unit tests for Alpine
- [x] Enable test lab runs of core-setup unit tests for Alpine

Enabling test lab runs depends on the lab infrastructure adding support for Alpine containers (tracked by https://github.com/dotnet/core-eng/issues/1799)

- [ ] Fix all remaining issues exposed by the lab test runs
- [x] Enable CLI repo building for Alpine

At this point, Alpine Linux can be targeted by CLI running directly on Alpine

issue_title: File.GetCreationTime  returns 01/01/0001 2:20:00 on ubuntu
HTTP code 302 (redirect) should not be considered as failure
issue_body:
Condition: spider received HTTP 302 response.

Actual behaviour: spider retries the same URL with different proxies until it gives up, and after it gave up, it visits the redirect URL

Expected behaviour: follow the redirect URL (either with the same or different proxy, does not matter. But I think the same proxy is the preferable behaviour)

I checked my settings and verified that I did not disable the [`RedirectMiddleware`](https://doc.scrapy.org/en/latest/topics/downloader-middleware.html#redirectmiddleware-settings)

issue_title: Let's Encrypt CA not included in Ubuntu's CA bundle
issue_body:
I can't connect with curl and wget to my server from Ubuntu after I installed the certificate issued by Let's Encrypt. Firefox and Opera do. I must turn off certificate validation to get them to connect.  The reason is that Let's Encrypt CA not included in Ubuntu's CA bundle. This might be distribution dependent because other distributions could already have Let's Encrypt in their list of CAs.
Tested on both Ubuntu 14.04 and 12.04. 

```
14.04 $ dpkg -l curl wget ca-certificates
ii  ca-certificates    20141019ubuntu0.12 Common CA certificates
ii  curl               7.22.0-3ubuntu4.14 Get a file from an HTTP, HTTPS or FTP server
ii  wget               1.13.4-2ubuntu1.2  retrieves files from the web

12.04 $ dpkg -l curl wget ca-certificates
ii  ca-certificates    20141019ubuntu all            Common CA certificates
ii  curl               7.35.0-1ubuntu amd64          command line tool for transferring data wi
ii  wget               1.15-1ubuntu1. amd64          retrieves files from the web
```

According to both OSes the 3 packages are "```already the newest version.```"
Given that Ubuntu is quite used on servers and that curl and wget could be used for server side automation tasks I suggest to work with the distributions and get Let's Encrypt added to the CAs bundle. Furthermore there are many other programs that fail to connect to Let's Encrypt sites possibly because of this reason. Example: this Ruby one liner fails

(I replaced the name of my server with example.com)
```
> HTTParty.get("https://www.example.com")
OpenSSL::SSL::SSLError: SSL_connect returned=1 errno=0 state=SSLv3 read server certificate B: certificate verify failed
...
```

This means that we can't use Let's Encrypt for sites that serve APIs consumed by other backend programs, unless we ask them to manually add Let's Encrypt to their CAs bundle.

Details of the curl and wget errors:

```
$ curl https://www.example.com

curl: (60) SSL certificate problem, verify that the CA cert is OK. Details:
error:14090086:SSL routines:SSL3_GET_SERVER_CERTIFICATE:certificate verify failed
More details here: http://curl.haxx.se/docs/sslcerts.html

curl performs SSL certificate verification by default, using a "bundle"
 of Certificate Authority (CA) public keys (CA certs). If the default
 bundle file isn't adequate, you can specify an alternate file
 using the --cacert option.
If this HTTPS server uses a certificate signed by a CA represented in
 the bundle, the certificate verification probably failed due to a
 problem with the certificate (it might be expired, or the name might
 not match the domain name in the URL).
If you'd like to turn off curl's verification of the certificate, use
 the -k (or --insecure) option.

$ wget https://www.example.com
--2015-12-26 11:34:56--  https://www.example.com
Resolving www.example.com (www.example.com)... x.x.x.x
Connecting to www.example.com (www.example.com)|x.x.x.x|:443... connected.
ERROR: cannot verify www.example.com's certificate, issued by `/C=US/O=Let\'s Encrypt/CN=Let\'s Encrypt Authority X1':
  Unable to locally verify the issuer's authority.
To connect to www.example.com insecurely, use `--no-check-certificate'.
```

issue_title: Domain order not preserved in generated CSR
issue_body:
4d6a1ee7ff23b29d4416ee91610a7d8a58173292 introduced a change which causes the domain order passed to the client not to be preserved.

The previous implementation in `get_authorizations` would iterate over the original domain list, preserving the original order, whereas the new implementation uses `authzr.values()`, which does not follow the same sorting order.

As a side-effect, the Common Name field is not guaranteed to be the first domain passed via `-d`, causing issues for users who are relying on this after upgrading to 0.5.0.

```
Attempting to renew cert from /tmp/leitrssL/conf/renewal/le.wtf.conf produced an unexpected error: day is out of range for month. Skipping.
```

issue_title: Conda packages are available on conda-forge
issue_body:
Just an FYI: Conda packages for `confluent-kafka-python` and `librdkafka` are available on conda-forge, a community-run build system for binary packages that is common among Python's data science community.  This allows Python users on Linux or OSX using Python 2 or 3 to download and install both packages with the following command: 

    conda-install -c conda-forge python-confluent-kafka

In case readers are unaware, conda-forge is used at essentially every Python tutorial at the Python data-science oriented conferences (pydata, scipy, strata, etc.) and now often serves as the source of recipes for the commercial Anaconda distribution.

### Three thoughts

1.  It might be useful for someone at Confluent to check over the build recipes to ensure that everything is as it should be.
2.  It might be useful to ensure that someone from Confluent has rights to update these package on the conda-forge build service in the future
3.  It might be useful to include this as an option within installation documentation


### Build recipes

-  https://github.com/conda-forge/librdkafka-feedstock/blob/master/recipe/meta.yaml
-  https://github.com/conda-forge/librdkafka-feedstock/tree/master/recipe

This is just an FYI, not a bug report.  Feel free to close.

issue_body:
https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/a5d860d370a8486fdbcd338c244dc5f583beba5f/dateutil/relativedelta.py#L377
```
end = datetime.datetime(2018, 8, 28) + relativedelta(months=+6)
print end
end = datetime.datetime(2018, 8, 29) + relativedelta(months=+6)
print end
end = datetime.datetime(2018, 8, 30) + relativedelta(months=+6)

```
both output

```
2019-02-28 00:00:00
2019-02-28 00:00:00
2019-02-28 00:00:00
```

I think it's not unreasonable to return the same date when different start_date add the same months.

issue_title: Wrong dst() value for time in Portugal tz
issue_body:
Pick a time in the Summer of 1996:
```
>>> t = datetime(1996, 6, 1, tzinfo=tz.gettz('Portugal'))
```
the time zone abbreviation is computed correctly:
```
>>> t.tzname()
'WEST'
```
([WEST](http://www.timeanddate.com/time/zones/west) = Western European Summer Time).

However, 

```
>>> t.dst()
datetime.timedelta(0)
```

and as a consequence:

```
>>> t.timetuple().tm_isdst
0
>>> from time import strftime
>>> strftime('%Z', t.timetuple())
'WET'
```

The problem is that `t.dst()` is computed as a difference between `t.utcoffset()` and the offset of the last standard time before `t`, but Portugal switched to `WEST` in the Spring of 1996 from `CET`, not `WET` and therefore UTC offset did not change, while isdst did.

issue_title: Two Way Serialization
issue_body:
Trying to cope with this without writing two schemes for the same stuff.

Let's say I'm taking in one API and it gives me something like this:

```
{ 
 "CamelCased": "Stuff",
 "somethingElse": "More stuff",
 "OhLookAList": [1,2,3,4]
}
```

And I'm repackaging it, doing some fun stuff internally, and then dumping it out the other side into my own API. Don't ask why I can't keep the same names. 

```
{
 "camel_cased": "That's a lie now.",
 "something_else": "More stuff",
 "oh_look_a_list": [1,2,3,4]
}
```
Normally I'd just use dump_to/load_from and be done with it all...however, I need to reverse the flow and send information from my API back to the external API. So someone will post:

```
{
 "camel_cased": "That's a lie now.",
 "something_else": "More stuff",
 "oh_look_a_list": [1,2,3,4]
}
```

And the external API will get...

```
{ 
 "CamelCased": "Stuff",
 "somethingElse": "More stuff",
 "OhLookAList": [1,2,3,4]
}
```

In the example text for marshmallow-enum I used a (nasty, in my opinion) pre_load/pre_dump workaround that looked at the schema context to see if the incoming data was the internal source or external source, and then switched a value on the field. However, I'm hesitant to do that for an *entire* schema.

The best way I can think to handle this with Marshmallow is to just some sort of schema factory that'd create external/internal schemas on demand, potentially a less crappy version of this:

```python
def make_two_way_schema(context):
     class SomeSchema(Schema):
         id = fields.Str(load_from=context['id']['read'], dump_to=context['id']['write'])
     return SomeSchema(...)
```

However, I'm unplussed about this idea or the prospect of managing two sets of schemas. I could see manufacturing the schemas before hand -- using the above mechanism -- and then at dump/load time choose the correct one. Maybe something like...


```python
def make_two_way_schema(mapping):
      internal_to_external = ...
      external_to_internal = ...
      
      def schema_choose(...): # all the same args as Schema.__init__
          return internal_to_external if context['source'] == 'internal' else external_to_internal
      return schema_choose
```

This strikes me as the lesser of two evils but not by much... Any ideas?

```
Collecting cloudpickle==0.4.0

    value = func(*args)
```

issue_title: serial.close() on a mac kills usb ports
issue_body:
I've verified this on osx 10.11.4 and 10.12.1. Whenever I close a Serial instance, and then unplug/replug in the usb ports, the usb port disappears. Here's the code I'm using:

`import serial
import sys
import glob

def serial_ports():
    """ Lists serial port names

        :raises EnvironmentError:
            On unsupported or unknown platforms
        :returns:
            A list of the serial ports available on the system
    """
    if sys.platform.startswith('win'):
        ports = ['COM%s' % (i + 1) for i in range(256)]
    elif sys.platform.startswith('linux') or sys.platform.startswith('cygwin'):
        # this excludes your current terminal "/dev/tty"
        ports = glob.glob('/dev/tty[A-Za-z]*')
    elif sys.platform.startswith('darwin'):
        ports = glob.glob('/dev/tty.usb*')
    else:
        raise EnvironmentError('Unsupported platform')

    result = []
    for port in ports:
        try:
            s = serial.Serial(port)
            s.close()
            result.append(port)
        except (OSError, serial.SerialException):
            pass
    return result

ports = serial_ports()
print ports
ser = serial.Serial(ports[0], 115200, timeout=1)
ser.close()
`

Interestingly, on 10.12.1, if I omit the ser.close() call, the problem does not happen. The problem still happens on 10.11.4 -- I expect that's for a different reason: 10.11.4 will kill usb ports that are disconnected while an active connection happens, whereas this does not happen for 10.12.1.
